params.text:
Bringing it together

You have learned that concussion is a mild traumatic brain injury caused by a blow or jolt to the head or body, that you cannot see it on the outside, and that in care it most often follows a fall. You know the signs to look for — headache, dizziness, confusion, nausea, blurred vision, memory problems, drowsiness and changes in behaviour — and that these can appear hours or days later, so the person must be monitored closely.

  • Recognise: spot the signs of concussion, even when there is no visible mark.
  • Respond: give basic first aid, keep the person still, calm and monitored.
  • Escalate: act on the red flags and take extra care with blood thinners.

Your key takeaways

You have seen when a head injury is an emergency — loss of consciousness, repeated vomiting, a seizure, weakness or numbness, clear fluid or bleeding from the ears or nose, a worsening or severe headache, or being difficult to wake — and that people on anticoagulants need extra caution. You know how to support recovery with rest and a gradual return to normal, to protect against a second injury, and to record and report the injury accurately while following the care plan.

This training gives you awareness and knowledge to help you recognise and respond to concussion and head injury. It does not replace supervised or first-aid training. Above all, remember that a serious head injury is a medical emergency that needs urgent medical help — if you ever see the red flags, call 999 at once.
